coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence nanoprobe for trace p-nitrophenol detection based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Liu, Yingnan
Wang, Qinzhi
Bu, Tong
Sun, Xinyu
Jia, Pei
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: P-nitrophenol (PNP) has been widely applied to industry processing for many purposes, but the persistence and toxicity of residuum may pose risks to human health. To analyze PNP in industrial and agricultural wastewater, a versatile fluorescent probe sensing platform was proposed. In this work, we devised a fluorescence approach that utilized nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs) to monitor PNP originating from the inner filter effect (IFE). The N,Si-CDs were generated in a one-step hydrothermal synthesis, and which possessed outstanding fluorescence signal and water-dispersity. Emission at 441 nm was monitored with excitation at 360 nm using a common spectrofluorometer. The method achieved an exceptionally low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.011 μM. Furthermore, this method not only eliminates the interference from metal ions and acid ions, but also provides a potential application prospect for N,Si-CDs in the field of water monitoring. Analysis of tap and lake water led to 93.30–106.30% recoveries and <1% relative standard deviation at 2.5–25 μM PNP concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P-nitrophenol
Nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent detection
Water samples

Synthesis and properties of the N,Si-CDs
Fluorescence response of N,Si-CDs towards PNP
Analysis of PNP in the actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A), Particle size distribution histogram (B), AFM images (C) and height distribution histogram (D) along the line overlaid in panel C, XRD pattern (E) and FT-IR spectra (F) of the synthesized N,Si-CDs.
XPS (A), high-resolution Si 2p (B), C 1s (C), N 1s (D), O 1s (E) spectra of N,Si-CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N,Si-CDs, PNP and PNP + N,Si-CDs. The illustration shows the color change of PNP added to N,Si-CDs solution at different concentrations under the sunlight (0–100 μM) (B) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of N,Si-CDs, the illustration shows picture of N,Si-CD solution under daylight and UV light.

Effect of PNP concentration (0–250 μM) on fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs (A). Picture of fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) at 441 nm versus the concentrations of PNP (B). The illustration shows the linear fitting curve of fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) and PNP concentration (0.025–80 μM).

Selectivity of N,Si-CDs-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of PNP. The interference ions are Fe3+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, Co2+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, Ac−, NO2 −, NO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Benzoic acid, ONP, MNP, Catechol, Hydroquinone, Resorcinol. The concentrations of all interferences are 80 μM.
Illustration of the synthetic process of N,Si-CDs and PNP sensing.
Comparison of the analytical performance of several commonly selected methods for the determination of PNP.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of PNP in real water samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The above results are the average of three repeated experiments.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence nanoprobe for trace p-nitrophenol detection based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P-nitrophenol (PNP) has been widely applied to industry processing for many purposes, but the persistence and toxicity of residuum may pose risks to human health. To analyze PNP in industrial and agricultural wastewater, a versatile fluorescent probe sensing platform was proposed. In this work, we devised a fluorescence approach that utilized nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs) to monitor PNP originating from the inner filter effect (IFE). The N,Si-CDs were generated in a one-step hydrothermal synthesis, and which possessed outstanding fluorescence signal and water-dispersity. Emission at 441 nm was monitored with excitation at 360 nm using a common spectrofluorometer. The method achieved an exceptionally low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.011 μM. Furthermore, this method not only eliminates the interference from metal ions and acid ions, but also provides a potential application prospect for N,Si-CDs in the field of water monitoring. Analysis of tap and lake water led to 93.30–106.30% recoveries and <1% relative standard deviation at 2.5–25 μM PNP concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: P-nitrophenol (PNP), is a class of aromatic phenolic compound containing nitro group, as an intermediate in industrial activities, has been known to play a vital role in many industrial processes and agrochemical products, including textiles, leather, paper, rubber, medicine, printing, plastic and dye manufacturing [1–3]. However, as a chemical industrial contaminant, PNP is featured by high stability, strong water solubility, poor biodegradability and widely exists in industrial and agricultural wastewater [4,5]. Even at very low concentration, PNP can cause irreversible harm to plants, animals and humans [3,6], which has become an increasingly serious global problem nowadays. Furthermore, PNP has been listed as priority pollutant to be detected and controlled in the water environment, and the maximum concentration limit should be less than 0.14 μM by US Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) [7–9]. Hence, it is urgently required to develop a simple, efficient, rapid and reliable method for detecting PNP in industrial and agricultural wastewater.

In the past few decades, a variety of analytical methods have been explored to detect PNP in water samples, such as capillary electrophoresis [10], electrochemical [11], spectrophotometric method [6], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12], fluorescence method [13] and so on. Among them, fluorometric methods have the advantages of simple operation, less sample consumption, rapid reaction, high sensitivity and good reproducibility. Fluorescence nanoprobe, including quantum dots [14], nanoplates [15], nanoparticles [16], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [17] and so on, which are expected to be used in a variety of biomedicine, environmental analysis, and food safety detection systems [18], have attracted extensive research interest in recent years. Among them, carbon dots, which are carbon nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm in size, have appealed to increasing attention in a variety of fields because of their outstanding biocompatibility, optical performance, drug delivery capacity, and low cytotoxicity [19,20]. Therefore, many researchers have developed other elemental doped carbon dots as a new material to explore their properties. For example, Zhang et al. successfully synthesized carbon dots using Bacillus cereus as a precursor to quickly and sensitively detect PNP in soil [21]. Han and his colleagues developed a reversible fluorescent nanoswitch for visual and selective detection of PNP in ambient and drinking water by changing the pH of the system [22]. Li and his team developed Chromium(III)-doped carbon dots with low cytotoxicity as a fluorescent sensing platform, demonstrating the feasibility of measuring PNP in human urine samples [23]. Although these methods can achieve certain applications, effective detection of analytes was limited by two common factors: (1) the selectivity of the fluorescent sensors was unsatisfactory, which was easily disturbed by metal ions and acid ions. (2) the variations of fluorescence signal were inconspicuous enough, and the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensors needs to be further improved. Therefore, it is essential for a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor to assess PNP residues.

To address the mentioned issues, a novel quenching fluorescence nanoprobe was designed and synthesized for detecting PNP based on nitrogen, silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs), which was shown in Scheme 1 . Compared with other nanoprobes for detecting PNP previously reported, the prepared N,Si-CDs possessed excellent water dispersity, high stability and exceptional optical properties. Under the UV light of 365 nm, the synthesized N,Si-CDs emerged strong blue fluorescence. After the addition of PNP, the blue fluorescence was quenched and can be clearly identified by the naked eyes. This result is largely due to inner filter effect (IFE) of the N,Si-CDs and PNP. A good linear relationship was obtained for PNP detection from 0.025 to 80 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.011 μM. In addition, this work not only eliminated the interference from metal ions and acid ions, but also furnished a potential prospect for N,Si-CDs in the field of industrial and agricultural wastewater monitoring.

Diethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was purchased from Adama reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PNP, N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO), hydroquinone, catechol, Fe(NO3)3, Ni(NO3)2, and MnCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Cr(NO3)3, KBr, Cu(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, benzoic and Hg(NO3)2 were supplied by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaNO2, NaNO3, KNO3, Ca(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Na2SO3, Na2SO4 and Na2PO4 were obtained from Guanghua technology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Pb(NO3)2, Na2S and NaAc were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Mg(NO3)2, m-nitrophenol (MNP) and resorcinol were purchased from Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). o-nitrophenol (ONP) was purchased from Shanghai McLean biochemical technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl was obtained from Sichuan Xi Long science co. Ltd. (Sichuan, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Twice-distilled water was used through all experiments.

The morphology of N,Si-CDs was characterized by Hitachi600 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Japan) and Nano Measurer software, respectively. The image of atomic force microscope (AFM) was carried out on NanoScope V instrument with tapping mode (Bruker, Germany). The N,Si-CDs solution was dripped onto a silicon wafer for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis using the Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a Bruker D8 Advanced Diffractometer System, which equipped with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 40 mA) ability. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was taken on Vetex 70 FT-IR spectroscopy (Germany). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Fluoromax-4 (USA) with a standard 10 mm path length colorimetric quartz tube. Luminescence lifetime measurements were collected with an Edinburgh FLS 1000 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

With DEHP and DAMO as precursors, N,Si-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method [24]. Briefly, DAMO (5 mL) was dissolved in water (20 mL) with ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. Then, DEHP (1 mL) was added to the above mixed system and allowed to stand for 10 min. Subsequently, the above solution was shifted to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling to 25 ± 2 °C, the resulting liquid was filtered by a membrane (0.22 μm) to eliminate biggish insolubles. The obtained yellow solution was purified with 100–500 Da dialysis bags for 12 h. Finally, the as-prepared N,Si-CDs solution was preserved in a 4 °C medical refrigerator.

The determination of PNP based on N,Si-CDs was carried out according to the following steps. In brief, N,Si-CDs (0.2 mL) solution was mixed with various concentrations of PNP (0.2 mL) from 0 to 250 μM. Then, the above solution was diluted to 0.8 mL with buffer solution (pH = 7) followed by reacting at 40 °C for 3 min to ensure complete reaction. Under excitation (360 nm), the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution were determined at the 441 nm. According to the preliminary experiment, we found that the fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) had a good linear relationship with the concentration of PNP, which was better than the relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and PNP concentration. Among them, F0 and F refer to the maximum value of the fluorescence curve before and after the addition of PNP to the N,Si-CDs solution, respectively. For Fe3+, Zn2+ or other interfering ions, PNP solution (0.2 mL) was replaced by the corresponded solution and the fluorescence measurement procedures were conducted by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PNP in the actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the reliability of the method, tap water and lake water were selected as the actual samples. Actual samples were acquired from West Lake water sample in Northwest A&F University, and local water samples from tap water. First, a 0.22 μm membrane was used to remove larger insoluble particles for further quantitative analysis. Then different concentrations of PNP (2.5 μM, 10 μM, 25 μM) were prepared in actual water with a standard solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra under 360 nm excitation were recorded.

All experiments were repeated 3 times, and the results were expressed as mean ± relative standard deviation (RSD). Data were analyzed and plotted using SPSS 20.0 and Origin 9.0. P ≤ 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and properties of the N,Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, diameter, structure of surface functional chemical composition and optical properties were investigated by HR-TEM, AFM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra respectively. As shown in Fig. 1A, the N,Si-CDs had good dispersity in water solution. The particle size histogram of about 100 nanoparticles was calculated and the average particle size is 5.4 nm (Fig. 1B). The planar structure of the synthesized N,Si-CDs was further measured by typical tapping AFM (Fig. 1C), with a preliminary estimate of thickness between 0.5 and 2.5 nm (Fig. 1D). The XRD pattern (Fig. 1E) showed an apparent diffraction peak at 21.8° corresponding to the interlayer spacing of the graphite structure [21].

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded to characterize the surface groups of N,Si-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1F, the three intense infrared bands at 3445 cm−1, 1608 cm−1and 1064 cm−1 were attributed to the OH stretching vibration [25], NH bending vibration [26] and SiOSi stretching vibration [27], respectively. The weak signals bands at 2932 cm−1, 2860 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 were assigned to the C-H(CH2 and CH3)group bending and stretching vibration [28,29]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1390 and 1342 cm−1 were attributed to COO in plane stretching vibration [30] and CN stretching vibration [31]. In addition, the characteristic peaks at 1313 cm−1, 771 cm−1and 921 cm−1 were assigned to the SiC asymmetric bending, SiC tensile vibration and the stretching vibration of the SiO group [24] or the SiOSi group [32]. These results confirmed that the subsistence of abundant amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and silicon-containing functional groups (SiO/SiC) on the surface of N,Si-CDs. These functional hydrophilic groups improve their water dispersity and were conducive to the application of fluorescent probes for detection in water systems.

The surface chemical bands and element composition of N,Si-CDs were characterized by XPS. Fig. 2A exhibited four major peaks at 101.52, 284.24, 398.54, and 531.34 eV, which corresponded to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with the elements proportion of 3.84%, 74.87%, 6.14% and 15.15%, respectively. The XPS spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. 2B) manifested the presence of SiC (101.5 eV), SiN (102.1 eV), and SiO (102.8 eV) groups, respectively [33]. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2C) manifested the presence of CSi (284.2 eV), CC/CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.3 eV), and NCO/OCO (288.9 eV), respectively [34]. From the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), three peaks were attributed to NSi (398.6 eV), CNC (399.3 eV), and N(C)3 (399.8 eV) groups [35]. In addition, the OSi bond (531.7 eV) and CO bond (532.3 eV) were observed from the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) [36]. These mentioned results confirmed the N,Si-CDs were prepared successfully and owed extensive functional groups, which was consistent with FT-IR.

The optical properties of N,Si-CDs had important significance to the detection of PNP. As a result of π-π* transition of CC bond [37,38], a broad UV–vis absorption band of N,Si-CDs was exhibited at 200–350 nm with distinct peaks at 230 and 308 nm (Fig. 3A). Moreover, a new UV absorption peak was located at 399 nm after the addition of PNP. Correspondingly, the color of reaction system solution changed from light yellow to bright yellow (The illustration of Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B exhibited that the N,Si-CDs had excitation (blue line) and emission peaks (red line) at 360 nm and 441 nm, respectively. The inset of Fig. 3B presented light yellow and intense blue fluorescence of N,Si-CD solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively. Consequently, the synthesized N,Si-CDs involved outstanding optical properties, which provided the support to structure a fluorescence (FL) quenching sensor. Subsequently, N,Si-CDs solution was stored at room temperature for 15 days to study its optical stability. As depicted in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs showed no significant difference at different storage times, displaying satisfactory photostability.

We found that N,Si-CDs had good dispersity in aqueous solution. As can be seen from Fig. S1B, the optimal excitation wavelength was 360 nm, which was ascribed to the band-to-band radiative recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes [39]. At the same time, it was found that with the augment of excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission peak was accompanied by an apparent red-shift. This phenomenon may be attributed to the quantum effect and the distribution of emissive taps on the surface of N,Si-CDs [40]. Compared with reported fluorescence sensor, the sensitivity of N,Si-CDs solution to PNP was much higher than that of its isomers MNP and ONP (Fig. S2A). This phenomenon provided strong evidence for the specific recognition and detection of PNP, which encouraged us to explore its application prospect in fluorescence detection.

In order to obtain the best experimental effect for the detection of PNP, the reaction temperature, time and pH were optimized during the detection process. It can be clearly seen from Fig. S2B that with the increase of reaction time, the fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) increased rapidly and then remained stable. However, the reaction temperature and buffer pH had different effects on fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F). As shown Fig. S2 C and D, the reaction temperature and pH corresponding to the maximum value of the fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) were 40 °C and pH = 7, respectively. Consequently, the optimal experimental conditions for PNP sensing were 5 min of reaction time and pH 7 at 40 °C for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N,Si-CDs towards PNP
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the fluorescence response of N,Si-CDs towards PNP, different concentrations of PNP were added to the N,Si-CDs solution. As displayed in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of the N,Si-CDs solution decreased gradually with the addition of PNP ranging from 0 to 250 μM, and the quenching effect was very obvious. The experimental results indicated that the N,Si-CDs solution had excellent performance in detecting PNP as fluorescent nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. 4B, a good linear relationship was obtained between the fluorescence quenching variable (F0-F) and the concentration of PNP in the range of 0.025–80 μM. The regression equation was F0-F = 85,134.850 + 16,108.316x (The slope and the intercept are the average of three replicates, R2 = 0.991), where x was the concentration of PNP, and the detection limit (LOD, refers to 3 times the ratio of the blank standard deviation to the slope of the standard curve) was 0.011 μM. In addition, the linear range of this method was comparable to other methods, and the detection limit was the lowest reported so far (Table 1 ). This result may be due to the fact that amino and carboxyl provided more active sites for binding to PNP in the reaction system [41], indicating that our detection system possesses better analysis ability than other fluorescent materials.

Selectivity is an important factor to evaluate the analytical performance of fluorescent nanoprobes, especially for the nanoprobes with potential application prospects in actual samples. In order to prevent the influence of some reactive ingredients such as metal ions for the specificity of PNP, the response of N,Si-CDs to different interfering species was assessed. As depicted in Fig. 5 , we investigated the influence of some possible interfering substances in the water environment system on the experimental results, including Fe3+, Zn2+, Cr2+, Na+, Co2+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cl−, Ac−, NO2 −, NO3 −, S2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, benzoic acid, ONP, MNP, catechol, hydroquinone, resorcinol. Compared to above interfering substances, the fluorescence signal of the nanoprobe had an obvious change towards PNP. The presence of phenolic derivative, metal ions and acid ions did not significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs solutions, except for the isomers of PNP, catechol and hydroquinone. However, considering the fact that PNP is the most common nitrophenol compound in environmental water, it can be inferred that the potential interference did not affect the detecting PNP to the fluorescent probe. The results showed that the fluorescence detection method had good selectivity to the PNP.

In order to elucidate the possible mechanism of the N,Si-CDs fluorescent nanoprobes for PNP detection, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,Si-CDs and N,Si-CDs-PNP mixtures were measured by steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. As depicted in the Fig. S3, we observed that their fluorescence intensity decay was conformed to exponential decay function. As listed in Table S1, the fluorescence lifetime was not obviously change with the addition of PNP (5.27 ns) comparing with the free N,Si-CDs (5.23 ns), indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N,Si-CDs by PNP may follow a simple static quenching mechanism or IFE mechanism [48]. Furthermore, the quenching effect can be expressed well by Stern-Volmer's equation (Supporting information and Fig. S4):
(1)
F 0
λ
/ F
λ
=
K sv
λ
Q
+ 1 =
K q
τ 0
Q
+ 1

It was found that the quenching rate constant (Kq) was greater than the maximum value of dynamic quenching effect (1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1) by calculation. This result further proved that the quenching mechanism was static quenching.

In addition, there was a spectrum overlap between the excitation of N,Si-CDs and the absorption of PNP (Fig. S5). Therefore, the quenching behaviors of fluorescent nanoprobes by the quencher were likely to follow the IFE because the possibility of IFE principally depends on the extent of overlap of quencher absorption spectrum and fluorophore excitation and/or emission [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of PNP in the actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the accuracy and practicability of this method, the N,Si-CDs probe were used by standard addition method for detecting PNP in lake water and tap water samples. The samples to be tested was filtered with a 0.22 um membrane, and the filtrate was applied for PNP detection. Subsequently, a series of different concentrations of PNP were dissolved to actual samples in the optimal conditions for the fluorescence detection, and the recovery rate was calculated. As listed in Table 2 , it was satisfactory that the recoveries of PNP were between 93.30% and 106.30%, and the general relative standard deviation were ranged from 0.55% to 0.93%.

In summary, we have successfully developed novel N,Si-CDs fluorescent nanoprobes for identification and determination of PNP. The fluorescent nanoprobes were proved to possess remarkable advantages, including high stability, outstanding water dispersity and intense blue fluorescence. The N,Si-CDs can be employed without any modification to detect PNP based on IFE effect. Furthermore, the method was also successfully applied for PNP detection in actual samples with satisfactory results. Significantly, this method provided a new way for constructing a sensing platform for detecting hazard factors of environmental water.

Xin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Yingnan Liu: Validation, Visualization. Qinzhi Wang: Validation, Visualization. Tong Bu: Validation, Visualization. Xinyu Sun: Validation, Visualization. Pei Jia: Validation, Visualization. Li Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601) and Talented Program (A279021724). Authors would like to thank the instrument shared platform of college of plant protection of Northwest A&F University, for the assistance in the TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118876.
